topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official letter lay spread across the worn oak desk, the ink stark and unforgiving. Maya traced the elegant script with a trembling finger, the silence of her small apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in her chest. The scholarship. The golden ticket to art school, the thing she had sacrificed everything for. Now, the words swam before her eyes, revealing a name that wasn't hers. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in her throat, tasting like dust. She pushed the paper away, the movement jerky, and stood up, pacing the narrow confines of the living room. Her hands began to grip the edge of the windowsill until her knuckles were white. Every sound—the distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed to press in, building a pressure behind her eyes that made focusing on anything impossible. She sank onto the floor, curling into a tight ball, the world narrowing down to the suffocating weight of the realization.

[story 2]

Liam stared at the acceptance notification glowing on his laptop screen, the bright light reflecting off the sheen of sweat on his forehead. He reread the fine print, his breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. It wasn't a clerical error; it was a complete misdirection. The funds, the prestigious grant, were designated for a student named Clara. A cold dread began to seep into his bones, a heavy, paralyzing chill. He slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing loudly in the otherwise quiet room. He walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent cityscape. His vision blurred at the edges. He started running his hands through his hair, a desperate, repetitive motion, trying to physically shake the confusing reality loose. The sheer volume of what he had to process—the betrayal, the lost opportunity, the sheer impossibility of it all—made his legs feel weak, unsteady beneath him.

[story 3]

"No. This can't be right." Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible above the frantic thumping of her own pulse. She kept flipping back through the application documents, her eyes darting frantically across the pages, searching for a misplaced signature, a typo that could explain the anomaly. The reality sinking in was slow, like deep water creeping up her ankles, pulling her down. The scholarship, the culmination of years of relentless study and sacrifice, belonged to someone else entirely. Her jaw tightened so hard she could feel the strain in her molars. She stood rigid by her cluttered bookshelf, breathing in short, shallow bursts, trying to anchor herself to the solid wood, but the ground seemed to tilt beneath her feet. A wave of nausea washed over her, and she had to press her palms flat against her temples, fighting the sudden, dizzying urge to simply collapse.
